Diamond Prices Analysis

TJ Cook

`3/16/26

Diamond Prices

This analysis explores the relationship between diamond weight (carat) and price using the ggplot2::diamonds dataset.

Question 1 — Filter & Count Table

We retain only diamonds whose color is D (best), G (middle-grade), or J (worst), and whose clarity is IF (best), VS1 (middle-grade), or I1 (worst).

diamonds_sub <- diamonds %>% filter(color %in% c("D", "G", "J"), clarity %in% c("I1", "VS1", "IF")) %>% mutate(color   = droplevels(color), clarity = droplevels(clarity))
diamonds_sub %>% count(color, clarity) %>% tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = clarity, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>% kable()
color I1 VS1 IF
D 42 705 73
G 150 2148 681
J 50 542 51

The filtered dataset contains 4442 diamonds spread across nine color–clarity combinations. IF diamonds are considerably rarer than VS1 or I1 diamonds across all three color grades. [Question 1 complete]

Question 2 — Price vs. Carat (Overall)

ggplot(diamonds_sub, aes(x = carat, y = price)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) + theme_minimal()

There is a clear positive relationship between carat weight and price — heavier diamonds are substantially more expensive. [Question 2 complete]

Question 3 — Price vs. Carat by Color

ggplot(diamonds_sub, aes(x = carat, y = price, color = color)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name    = "Color\n(D = best, J = worst)") + theme_minimal()

All three color grades show the same positive price–carat trend, but there are differences. D-color (best) diamonds trend line sits highest. G-color diamonds fall in the middle, and J-color (worst) diamonds fetch the lowest prices per carat. [Question 3 complete]

Question 4 — Price vs. Carat by Color & Clarity

ggplot(diamonds_sub, aes(x = carat, y = price, color    = color, shape    = clarity, linetype = clarity)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) + scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name    = "Color\n(D = best, J = worst)") + theme_minimal()

Mapping clarity to both point shape and line type results in a difficult-to-read plot. [Question 4 complete]

Question 5 — Faceted Grid: Color × Clarity

ggplot(diamonds_sub, aes(x = carat, y = price)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.9) + facet_grid(color ~ clarity, labeller = labeller(color = c(D = "Color D (best)", G = "Color G (mid)", J = "Color J (worst)"), clarity = c(I1  = "Clarity I1 (worst)", VS1 = "Clarity VS1 (mid)", IF  = "Clarity IF (best)")))  + theme_minimal(base_size = 10) + theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

All panels show a positive price–carat relationship. The D–IF combination (top-right panel) has the steepest slope and the fewest data points. Conversely, J–I1 (bottom-left) has the shallowest slope. In general, moving toward better color and better clarity steepens the price-per-carat. [Question 5 complete]

Question 6 — Average Price per Carat by Color & Clarity

diamonds_ppc <- diamonds_sub %>% mutate(price_per_carat = price / carat)

ppc_summary <- diamonds_ppc %>% group_by(color, clarity) %>% summarize(avg_price_per_carat = round(mean(price_per_carat), 2), n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>% arrange(desc(avg_price_per_carat))

kable(ppc_summary, col.names = c("Color", "Clarity", "Avg Price per Carat (USD)", "N"))
Color Clarity Avg Price per Carat (USD) N
D IF 9937.42 73
G VS1 4420.88 2148
D VS1 4204.89 705
G IF 4007.01 681
J VS1 3750.35 542
J IF 3220.23 51
D I1 3064.48 42
J I1 2710.08 50
G I1 2617.48 150

The table largely agrees with the visual impressions from Questions 3 and 5. D–IF (best color, best clarity) tops the ranking with the highest average price per carat, confirming what the facet plot suggested. J–I1 (worst color, worst clarity) sits near the bottom. One noteworthy quirk: within a given color grade, IF clarity does not always rank above VS1 in price-per-carat — this can happen because the IF cells have very few observations and may skew toward smaller stones that don’t yet command their full premium. Overall, the numeric ranking is consistent with the graphical story: better quality diamonds fetch more per carat.1 ```

  1. Question 6 complete↩︎
